role_summary: South Carolina civil rights photographer; documented school desegregation, the 1968 Orangeburg Massacre, and the Charleston hospital workers' strike.
biographical_paragraph: By fourteen, Cecil J. Williams was already shooting for JET magazine, selected as one of twenty-five young freelance photographers the publication kept on call worldwide in the early 1950s [src: 1]. He was born November 26, 1937 in Orangeburg, South Carolina, where his lens would document a generation of the state's civil rights movement [src: 1]. His photograph of Harvey Gantt walking into Clemson University in 1963 sits in the visual record of school desegregation, and his images of the Orangeburg Massacre five years later sit in the record of state violence against student protest [src: 1]. In 1969 a frame he made of Coretta Scott King at the Charleston hospital workers' strike ran on the cover of JET [src: 1]. Williams was the official photographer for the South Carolina branch of the NAACP for more than two decades beginning in the 1960s, took an art degree at Claflin University in 1960, and in 2019 opened the Cecil Williams South Carolina Civil Rights Museum [src: 1]. South Carolina recognized him with the Order of the Palmetto in 2017; in 2015 he had patented a film-negative digitization device of his own design he calls the FilmToaster [src: 1]. The Library of Congress holds his oral history as entry 14 of the Civil Rights History Project [src: 2].

sources.title: Cecil J. Williams oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress
